questions.text: Hormones are non-nutrient chemicals which act as:
questions.explanation: Hormones are produced in trace amounts and communicate between different organs.

questions.text: The hypothalamus is the basal part of the:
questions.explanation: It contains groups of neurosecretory cells called nuclei which produce hormones.

questions.text: Which hormone is NOT produced by the anterior pituitary?
questions.explanation: Oxytocin and Vasopressin are synthesized by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary.

questions.text: Gigantism and Dwarfism are related to the over-secretion and under-secretion of:
questions.explanation: Growth Hormone imbalance affects the stature of an individual.

questions.text: The hormone that stimulates the growth of mammary glands and formation of milk is:
questions.explanation: Prolactin regulates the growth of the mammary glands and milk production.

questions.text: In females, LH (Luteinizing Hormone) induces:
questions.explanation: LH triggers ovulation of fully mature follicles and maintains the corpus luteum.

questions.text: Vasopressin (ADH) acts mainly at the kidney and stimulates:
questions.explanation: It reduces water loss through urine (diuresis).

questions.text: The pineal gland secretes a hormone called ________ which regulates the 24-hour diurnal rhythm.
questions.explanation: Melatonin helps in maintaining the sleep-wake cycle and body temperature.

questions.text: Which element is essential for the normal rate of hormone synthesis in the thyroid?
questions.explanation: Iodine deficiency leads to goitre (enlargement of the thyroid gland).

questions.text: Exophthalmic goitre is a form of:
questions.explanation: Also called Graves' disease, it is an autoimmune disorder leading to overproduction of thyroid hormones.

questions.text: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) increases ________ levels in the blood.
questions.explanation: PTH is a hypercalcemic hormone; it stimulates bone resorption to release calcium into blood.

questions.text: The 'Throne of Immunity' or the gland that plays a major role in the development of the immune system is:
questions.explanation: Thymus secretes thymosins which help in the differentiation of T-lymphocytes.

questions.text: Adrenaline and Noradrenaline are commonly called:
questions.explanation: They are secreted in response to stress or emergency (fight or flight).

questions.text: Glucocorticoids, like cortisol, are involved in:
questions.explanation: Cortisol stimulates gluconeogenesis and is also an anti-inflammatory agent.

questions.text: The main mineralocorticoid in our body is:
questions.explanation: Aldosterone regulates the balance of water and electrolytes in our body.

questions.text: Islets of Langerhans are found in the:
questions.explanation: The pancreas is a composite gland (both exocrine and endocrine).

questions.text: Alpha cells and Beta cells of the pancreas secrete ________ and ________ respectively.
questions.explanation: Glucagon increases blood sugar, while insulin decreases it.

questions.text: Diabetes mellitus is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Prolonged hyperglycemia leads to glucose loss in urine and ketone body formation.

questions.text: Leydig cells (interstitial cells) produce a group of hormones called:
questions.explanation: The main androgen is testosterone, responsible for male secondary sexual characteristics.

questions.text: Which hormone is known as the 'Pregnancy hormone'?
questions.explanation: Progesterone supports pregnancy and acts on mammary glands to stimulate milk secretion.

questions.text: The hormone erythropoietin, which stimulates RBC formation, is produced by:
questions.explanation: Kidneys have an endocrine function in regulating red blood cell production.

questions.text: Atrial Natriuretic Factor (ANF) is secreted by the heart to:
questions.explanation: ANF causes vasodilation to lower blood pressure.

questions.text: Hormones like Insulin and Glucagon are chemically:
questions.explanation: Most pancreatic and pituitary hormones are proteinaceous.

questions.text: Second messengers like cAMP or IP3 are typically used by ________ hormones.
questions.explanation: Protein hormones cannot enter the target cell, so they interact with membrane receptors and use second messengers.

questions.text: Steroid hormones regulate gene expression by interacting with:
questions.explanation: Steroids are lipid-soluble and can cross the cell membrane to bind with receptors in the cytoplasm or nucleus.

questions.text: Which of the following is an amino-acid derivative hormone?
questions.explanation: Epinephrine (Adrenaline) is derived from the amino acid tyrosine.

questions.text: CCK (Cholecystokinin) acts on:
questions.explanation: It stimulates secretion of pancreatic enzymes and bile juice.

questions.text: Addison's disease is caused by the underproduction of hormones from the:
questions.explanation: It leads to altered carbohydrate metabolism and acute weakness.

questions.text: Which hormone causes the contraction of smooth muscles of the uterus during childbirth?
questions.explanation: Oxytocin is often called the 'birth hormone'.

questions.text: The pituitary gland is located in a bony cavity called:
questions.explanation: Sella turcica is a depression in the sphenoid bone of the skull.

questions.text: MSH (Melanocyte Stimulating Hormone) acts on melanocytes to regulate:
questions.explanation: It is secreted by the pars intermedia of the pituitary.

questions.text: Thyrocalcitonin (TCT) secreted by the thyroid gland:
questions.explanation: TCT is a hypocalcemic hormone, acting opposite to PTH.

questions.text: Cretinism is caused by ________ during pregnancy.
questions.explanation: It leads to stunted growth and mental retardation in the child.

questions.text: The part of the adrenal gland that secretes Adrenaline is the:
questions.explanation: The adrenal medulla produces catecholamines.

questions.text: Glucagon is a ________ hormone.
questions.explanation: It stimulates glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to increase blood sugar.

questions.text: Which gland regresses (disappears) with age in humans?
questions.explanation: This is why immune responses of old persons become weak.

questions.text: Testes are located in the:
questions.explanation: The scrotum maintains a lower temperature necessary for spermatogenesis.

questions.text: Ovaries produce which of the following?
questions.explanation: They are the primary female sex organs.

questions.text: The 'master gland' of the endocrine system is usually considered the ________, although it is controlled by the hypothalamus.
questions.explanation: It regulates many other endocrine glands.

questions.text: Hypoglycemia is a condition of:
questions.explanation: Excessive insulin or insufficient glucagon can lead to this state.
